explicit_text: I was born and have lived most of my life in Barre, Vermont. It's a town known for its granite quarries, where many of the locals, including my husband and roomers, worked as stonecutters. Life here revolves around the quarries, though the work can be dangerous and seasonal, with slow periods especially in winter. Despite its challenges, Barre has good stores and enough liveliness for families, but not many jobs for young people after high school, which is why my sons found work elsewhere.

explicit_text: My mother-in-law came from Scotland and ran a rooming and boarding house for quarrymen here in Barre. She and her husband, who worked in the quarries, managed to buy the house through their hard work and thriftiness. Scotland is part of my family's heritage, especially through my husband Johnny, who was their only child. Though I was born in Barre, the Scottish roots are an important part of our family story.

explicit_text: Many of the stonecutters in Barre are French or of French descent, like my husband who came from Chambly, Quebec. The French stonecutters often have large families and face the hardships of quarry life, including the risk of tuberculosis. I know a Frenchman who's been working in the quarries since 1892, and my daughter is dating a French boy whose family has suffered from the disease. The French community here is tightly knit and resilient despite the dangers of the work.

explicit_text: My husband came down from Chambly, Quebec, with his family when he was a boy. Chambly was a farming community, and his father eventually returned to farming after a few years working in the granite sheds here. One of my daughters married a French boy from Chambly, and they now live there with her grandparents. Chambly is an important part of our family's background and connection to the French-Canadian stonecutting community.

explicit_text: My husband worked as a letter carver for seventeen years, and for one year he worked in Montpelier, Vermont. Many young people from Barre, including some of my sons' friends, find office jobs in Montpelier because there aren't enough opportunities here. Montpelier represents a nearby city where people seek work beyond the quarries. It's part of the regional landscape that shapes our lives and employment options.

explicit_text: After Memorial Day, the quarries usually have a two-week rest period, which is a regular part of the work cycle. This spring, work has been slow, and my roomer Alex is only working three days a week. Memorial Day marks a seasonal pause in quarry work, giving the men some time off after the holiday. It's a familiar rhythm that affects the whole community here in Barre.

explicit_text: I know that some of the stonecutters here are Spanish, and they have their own experiences with the hard work and dangers of quarry life. For example, the boy my daughter is going with lost his father and two brothers to tuberculosis, which is common among the stonecutters regardless of nationality. The Spanish workers share in the struggles and the union efforts to improve conditions. They are part of the diverse community that makes up the granite workforce in Barre.

explicit_text: Though I haven't lived near the Ebro River, I know it because of the Spanish stonecutters who come from that region. The Ebro River area is known in Spain, and some of the men here have roots there. Their stories and backgrounds from places like the Ebro River add to the rich tapestry of people working in the quarries. It reminds me how far some have traveled to work in Barre's granite industry.

explicit_text: Zaragoza is a city in Spain that some of the Spanish stonecutters here might come from or near. While I don't know many details, I understand that men from Zaragoza and other parts of Spain have come to work in Barre's quarries. Their presence here shows how the granite industry draws workers from many places, including Zaragoza. It's part of the broader story of immigration and labor in this town.
